summary: Fana is the Sufi doctrine of the passing away or annihilation of the ego-self before God, in which the mystic's awareness of his own separate selfhood is effaced in the consciousness of the divine reality. It is classically paired with baqa', the subsistence or abiding of the servant in God that follows it.
description:
In the vocabulary of Sufism (tasawwuf), fana' denotes the effacement of the ego-self and its attributes as the wayfarer approaches God, a state Sufi authors describe as the passing away of all consciousness of self so that only God remains present to the heart. It is treated in the manuals as one term of a pair: fana', the annihilation of the self, and baqa', the subsistence or abiding that follows, in which the servant returns to ordinary awareness while remaining established in God. Sufi writers distinguished degrees and objects of the annihilation, for example the passing away of blameworthy human attributes, the passing away of the wayfarer's acts in the vision of God as their true agent, and the effacement of the very consciousness of self.

The tradition characteristically contrasts two registers in which fana' was expressed. Al-Junayd al-Baghdadi (d. c. 910) is associated with a 'sober' (sahw) framing that read annihilation in the light of the primordial covenant and insisted on the return to the duties of the religious law, keeping mystical experience within its bounds. Abu Yazid al-Bistami (d. c. 875) is associated with an 'intoxicated' (sukr) register and with ecstatic utterances (shatahat), sayings uttered in a state of overpowering nearness that were read by some as expressions of union and by critics as transgressive; a comparable controversy attaches to the utterance ascribed to al-Hallaj. Later commentators, drawing on the school of Ibn Arabi, related fana' to their wider metaphysics of the divine names and the relation of God to the world. This entry reports these positions as attributed to their holders and does not adjudicate whether any formulation of fana' amounts to union with God or its denial.
editorial_notes: Scope: fana' as a technical term of Sufi psychology and its pairing with baqa'; the entry names the sober/intoxicated (sahw/sukr) contrast and the ecstatic utterances (shatahat) as descriptive schemes used by the tradition and by scholarship, not as endorsements, per neutrality principles #2, #4 and #7. Whether particular ecstatic utterances or formulations of fana' assert union (ittihad) with God is precisely the contested point between Sufi defenders and their critics; the entry attributes the positions and leaves the dispute unadjudicated. Attributions of a 'sober' register to al-Junayd and an 'intoxicated' one to al-Bistami follow the tradition's own and the scholarly literature's characterizations. Relations to sufism, al-junayd and baqa' are used; baqa' is a dangling target (no entry yet), which is permitted. Sources triangulate three lenses per neutrality principle #8: (a) a modern Western academic monograph on the technical vocabulary of the path (Chittick, SUNY 1989, which discusses annihilation and subsistence as a paired actualization); (b) the tradition's own classical primary manual, al-Qushayri's Risala, which sets out fana'/baqa' among the states and stations; and (c) a contemporary Muslim scholar (Nasr, Garden of Truth). All three were web-verified as existing works treating this material; none was read line-by-line in this pass, so confidence is medium.

relations.note: A technical term of the Sufi path (tasawwuf), naming the effacement of the ego-self before God.
Fana' (annihilation) is classically paired with baqa' (subsistence), the abiding in God that follows it (dangling target).
Associated with al-Junayd's 'sober' (sahw) framing of annihilation, keeping mystical experience within the bounds of the religious law.
Sufi authors relate fana' to the realization of the divine oneness (tawhid) in the wayfarer's consciousness; the interpretation is contested and here left unadjudicated.

historical_period.note: The vocabulary of fana'/baqa' crystallized in the formative Sufism of 3rd/9th-century Baghdad and Khurasan (al-Junayd, al-Bistami) and was systematized in the manuals of the 5th/11th century (al-Qushayri) and after.

sources.citation: Chittick, William C. The Sufi Path of Knowledge: Ibn al-'Arabi's Metaphysics of Imagination. Albany, NY: State University of New York Press, 1989. Treats annihilation (fana') and subsistence (baqa') as a paired opposition that must be actualized together within Ibn 'Arabi's technical vocabulary.
sources.license: Proprietary, cited for reference; text not reproduced.

sources.citation: al-Qushayri, Abu 'l-Qasim. al-Risala al-Qushayriyya fi 'ilm al-tasawwuf (5th/11th c.), trans. Alexander D. Knysh as Al-Qushayri's Epistle on Sufism. Reading: Garnet Publishing, 2007. Classical Sufi manual expounding the states and stations, including fana' and baqa'.
sources.license: Primary source in scholarly translation; cited for reference, relevant passages not read line-by-line.

sources.citation: Nasr, Seyyed Hossein. The Garden of Truth: The Vision and Promise of Sufism, Islam's Mystical Tradition. New York: HarperOne, 2007. A contemporary Muslim scholar's exposition of Sufism, treating the effacement of the self and the goal of the path; added as the modern-Muslim lens. Relevant passages not read line-by-line.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference, relevant passages not read line-by-line.
